stem: Let \( f(n) = [[p]]n^{[[a]]} \) and \( g(n) = [[q]]n^{[[b]]} \). To determine whether \( f(n) = O(g(n)) \) is a strict bound, evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} \).
solution: Since \( g \) has the strictly larger exponent (\( [[b]] > [[a]] \)), \( \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}n^{[[a]]-[[b]]}\to 0 \) as \( n\to\infty \). The limit is \( [[answer]] \), confirming \( f(n)=o(g(n)) \).
distractor_rationales: This is just the ratio of the leading coefficients, ignoring that the exponents differ.
This is the reciprocal of the coefficient ratio, ignoring the exponents entirely.
This assumes the two functions grow at the same rate.
This is the difference of the exponents rather than the value of the limit.
This confuses the exponent ratio with the limit of the function ratio.
This is the reciprocal of the exponent ratio, still not the limit.

stem: Let \( f(n) = [[p]]n^{[[k]]} + n \) and \( g(n) = [[q]]n^{[[k]]} \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} \) to determine whether \( f \) and \( g \) belong to the same growth order \( \Theta(n^{[[k]]}) \).
solution: The lower-order term \( n \) vanishes relative to \( n^{[[k]]} \), so \( \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} \to \frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]} = [[answer]] \), a finite nonzero limit — the two functions are of the same growth order.
distractor_rationales: This is the reciprocal of the correct coefficient ratio.
This assumes the two functions must grow at exactly the same rate with no scaling.
This multiplies the coefficients instead of dividing them.
This subtracts the coefficients instead of dividing them.
This adds the coefficients instead of dividing them.
This uses only the denominator's coefficient rather than the true ratio.

stem: Let \( f(n) = n^{[[k]]} \) and \( g(n) = [[c]]^{n} \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} \) to compare the growth of the polynomial \( f \) against the exponential \( g \).
solution: Exponential growth always dominates polynomial growth, so \( \frac{n^{[[k]]}}{[[c]]^n}\to [[answer]] \) as \( n\to\infty \): the exponential \( g \) grows strictly faster.
distractor_rationales: This treats the exponent [[k]] and the base [[c]] as if they combined into a simple ratio.
This is the reciprocal of the previous incorrect ratio.
This assumes the polynomial and exponential grow at comparable rates.
This evaluates an unrelated power instead of the limit of the ratio.
This subtracts the base from the exponent instead of taking the limit.
This subtracts the exponent from the base instead of taking the limit.

stem: Let \( f(n) = [[p]]\cdot [[a]]^{n} \) and \( g(n) = [[q]]\cdot [[b]]^{n} \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} \) to compare the two exponential growth rates.
solution: Since \( [[b]] > [[a]] \), the ratio \( \left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^n \to 0 \), so \( \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} = \frac{[[p]]}{[[q]]}\left(\frac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}\right)^n \to [[answer]] \): the larger base [[b]] dominates.
distractor_rationales: This is just the ratio of the leading coefficients, ignoring that the bases differ.
This is the ratio of the bases, mistaken for the limit itself.
This assumes the two exponentials grow at the same rate.
This is the reciprocal of the base ratio, still not the limit.
This is the reciprocal of the coefficient ratio, ignoring the bases.
This is the difference of the bases rather than the value of the limit.

stem: Let \( f(n) = [[p]]\left(\log n\right)^{[[k]]} \) and \( g(n) = [[q]]n^{[[m]]} \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{f(n)}{g(n)} \) to compare the growth of the logarithmic function \( f \) against the polynomial \( g \).
solution: Every positive power of \( n \) eventually outgrows every power of \( \log n \), so \( \frac{[[p]](\log n)^{[[k]]}}{[[q]]n^{[[m]]}}\to [[answer]] \) as \( n\to\infty \): the polynomial \( g \) grows strictly faster.
distractor_rationales: This is just the ratio of the leading coefficients, ignoring the log vs. polynomial comparison.
This treats the exponents [[k]] and [[m]] as if they directly gave the limit.
This assumes the logarithmic and polynomial functions grow at comparable rates.
This is the reciprocal of the exponent ratio, still not the limit.
This is the reciprocal of the coefficient ratio, ignoring the exponents.
This is the difference of the exponents rather than the value of the limit.
